title: The Stranger on the Road
subtitle: Two disciples walk to Emmaus, grieving, and the risen Lord joins them unrecognized

stillness: Be still, and let the road grow quiet.
You are walking, and your heart is heavy.
Speak of him softly, and wait — for he draws near to those who speak of him.
gospel: On that very day of the Lord's Resurrection,
behold, two of the disciples were going to a village called Emmaus,
some sixty furlongs from Jerusalem.
And they were talking together of all that had happened,
of his life among them and of his death,
and they grieved bitterly,
for they knew he had been killed innocent and without fault.
And it came to pass, while they talked and questioned together,
that Jesus himself drew near and went with them.
But their eyes were held, that they should not know him.
And he said: What are these words you exchange as you walk, and why are you sad?
One of them, named Cleophas, answered:
Are you the only stranger in Jerusalem who has not known what was done there in these days?
And he said: What things?
They said: Concerning Jesus of Nazareth,
a prophet mighty in deed and word before God and all the people;
how our chief priests and rulers delivered him to be condemned to death,
and crucified him.
But we had hoped that it was he who should redeem Israel.
Then he said: O foolish, and slow of heart to believe!
Did not the Christ have to suffer these things,
and so enter into his glory?
And beginning at Moses and all the Prophets,
he opened to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning himself.
behold: Attend to each thing, as if you were present there.
Behold the two disciples, going out from Jerusalem in their sorrow,
talking together as men who no longer wait for a living Christ,
but grieve as though the Saviour were destroyed.
Behold the Lord Jesus drawing near,
joining himself to them — in body and in spirit —
walking with them as a companion of the road,
asking and answering, sharing healing words.
Within their hearts they loved him; of his Resurrection they doubted —
and so he appeared to them outwardly in the body,
yet they did not recognize who he was.
Their eyes were held, not by falsehood, but by their own doubt:
because they doubted, the one they saw they did not know.
A stranger they thought him, whose face they did not recognize —
yet truly he was a stranger to them, who in their frailty stood far from the glory of his rising.
consider: Consider here the manifold kindness and goodness of the Lord.
First: his burning love could not bear to see his own so grieved and so astray.
Truly he is a faithful friend, a faithful companion, a kind Lord.
He joins himself to them, he seeks the cause of their sorrow,
he opens to them the Scriptures, and sets their hearts on fire.
So he does with us each day in spirit.
If, weighed down by some perplexity or weariness, we speak of him devoutly,
at once he is present — strengthening our hearts, enlightening them, kindling love.
For this is the best remedy against such troubles: to speak of God, and to think of him.
After the example of these disciples, when we walk along the way, let us speak of saving things,
that we may have Christ journeying with us —
and not only journeying, but teaching us, as he opened the Scriptures to the two.
It is good everywhere to speak of Jesus; good to hold him always in mind,
for neither does he forget those who are mindful of him.
And those who speak of his Passion he accompanies the more closely,
for the remembrance of what he suffered for us is more pleasing to him than all.
prayer: Lord Jesus, faithful friend, faithful companion, kind Lord,
you could not bear to see your own so grieved and so astray.
When I am weighed down by perplexity and weariness,
and speak of you devoutly upon the road,
be at once present to me.
Strengthen my heart and enlighten it,
and set it aflame with love of you.
Journey with me, and do not only journey,
but teach me, opening the Scriptures of yourself,
that my eyes, now held by doubt, may know you.
For you do not forget those who are mindful of you;
and those who remember your Passion you accompany the more closely.
Be in the midst of us, as you promised,
where two or three are gathered in your name. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Sic quotidie facit nobiscum spiritualiter: si enim aliqua perplexitate vel accidia gravati pie de ipso loquimur, statim adest, confortans corda nostra et illuminans, et etiam ad amorem suum inflammans.
latinJewel.english: So he does with us each day in spirit: for if, weighed down by some perplexity or listlessness, we speak of him devoutly, at once he is present, strengthening our hearts and enlightening them, and setting them aflame with love of himself.
